name: WhatsApp
overview:
WhatsApp is a messaging platform supporting 1-on-1 and group chats, media sharing, voice/video calls, and
end-to-end encryption. The system must deliver messages reliably (at-least-once delivery), support offline
message queuing (deliver when recipient comes online), handle group messages with thousands of members,
and maintain end-to-end encryption. WhatsApp serves 2B+ users with 100B+ messages per day.

inScope: Send and receive text messages (1-on-1 and groups)

items: Message delivery latency <500ms for online recipients
At-least-once delivery guarantee; messages must not be lost
99.99% availability — messaging outages are immediately user-visible
Offline message queue retained for up to 30 days
End-to-end encryption must not be compromised by server-side components

entities.name: Message
entities.schema:
{
  message_id: UUID,
  chat_id: string,
  sender_id: string,
  content: bytes,  // encrypted
  content_type: enum(TEXT|IMAGE|VIDEO|AUDIO|DOCUMENT),
  timestamp: ISO8601,
  status: enum(SENT|DELIVERED|READ)
}

entities.name: Chat
entities.schema:
{
  chat_id: string,
  type: enum(DIRECT|GROUP),
  participants: [string],
  last_message_id: UUID,
  created_at: timestamp
}

approaches.title: HTTP Request-Response
approaches.description: Client sends message via HTTP POST. Server stores and delivers to recipient. HTTP is stateless — server cannot push messages to recipient. Recipient must poll. High latency and server load.
approaches.tradeoff: No server push; polling required; high latency.

approaches.title: WebSocket with In-Memory Routing
approaches.description: Each client maintains a WebSocket connection. Server routes messages between connections. Works for single server but doesn't scale. With multiple servers, sender and recipient may be on different servers.
approaches.tradeoff: Doesn't scale across multiple servers; no offline delivery.

approaches.title: WebSocket + Message Queue for Offline Delivery
approaches.description: Each client maintains a WebSocket connection to a chat server. Chat servers are stateless — they route messages via a message queue. When recipient is online: message is delivered directly via WebSocket. When offline: message is stored in a per-user message queue (Redis list with TTL = 30 days). On reconnect, the client fetches all queued messages. Delivery receipts are sent back via WebSocket.
approaches.tradeoff: Redis message queue requires persistence; 30-day TTL may not be sufficient for all users.

approaches.title: Fan-out on Write
approaches.description: When a message is sent to a group, immediately deliver to all members. For a group with 1000 members, one message triggers 1000 deliveries. At 100B messages/day with average group size 50, this is 5 trillion deliveries/day.
approaches.tradeoff: Fan-out storm for large groups; prohibitive at scale.

approaches.title: Fan-out via Kafka with Per-Member Queues
approaches.description: Group message is published to Kafka. A fan-out service consumes the message and writes it to each member's message queue. Fan-out is asynchronous and parallelized. For large groups (>256 members), fan-out is rate-limited to prevent queue flooding. WhatsApp limits group size to 1024 members to bound fan-out cost.
approaches.tradeoff: Fan-out latency increases with group size; Kafka adds operational complexity.

components.name: Chat Servers
components.responsibility: Maintain WebSocket connections; route messages; manage delivery receipts

components.name: Message Queue (Redis)
components.responsibility: Per-user offline message queue; TTL = 30 days; delivers on reconnect

components.name: Kafka
components.responsibility: Group message fan-out; message persistence; delivery receipt events

components.name: Media Service
components.responsibility: Handles media uploads; stores in S3; generates thumbnails

components.name: User Presence Service
components.responsibility: Tracks online/offline status; last seen timestamp

components.name: Message DB (Cassandra)
components.responsibility: Stores message history; partition key = chat_id; clustering key = timestamp

components.name: Push Notification Service
components.responsibility: Sends APNs/FCM notifications when user is offline

questions.title: 2. Message Ordering
questions.content:
Messages within a chat must be delivered in order. Each message has a client-generated timestamp and a
server-assigned sequence number. The sequence number is monotonically increasing per chat. If messages
arrive out of order (due to network issues), the client buffers them and displays them in sequence number
order. The server assigns sequence numbers atomically using a Redis counter per chat_id.

questions.title: 3. Delivery Receipts
questions.content:
WhatsApp shows three receipt states: one tick (sent to server), two ticks (delivered to recipient's device), two
blue ticks (read by recipient). Sent: server acknowledges receipt. Delivered: recipient's device acknowledges
receipt via WebSocket. Read: recipient opens the chat and sends a read receipt. Receipts are stored in
Cassandra and pushed to the sender via WebSocket.

questions.title: 4. Media Delivery
questions.content:
Media files are uploaded to S3 via the Media Service. The media_id is included in the message. The recipient
downloads the media from S3/CDN when they open the message. Media is encrypted client-side before upload
(end-to-end encrypted). The server stores only the encrypted media blob — it cannot decrypt it. Media is
deleted from S3 after 30 days if not downloaded.

questions.title: 5. Presence and Last Seen
questions.content:
The User Presence Service tracks online/offline status. When a user connects, their status is set to ONLINE in
Redis. When they disconnect, it's set to OFFLINE with a last_seen timestamp. Presence updates are published
to Kafka and pushed to the user's contacts via WebSocket. Users can configure privacy settings to hide their
last seen timestamp.

keyFlows: ① User A sends message → Chat Server → route to User B (if online)
② User B offline: message stored in Redis offline queue (30-day TTL)
③ User B reconnects → Chat Server delivers queued messages
④ Group message → Kafka → fan-out to all member queues
⑤ Media: encrypted upload to S3 → media_id in message → recipient downloads

whatToStudyFurther.content:
Expert Note: Study the Signal Protocol: the X3DH key agreement protocol, the Double Ratchet algorithm
for forward secrecy, and how WhatsApp implements group encryption using sender keys.
Expert Note: Learn about WebSocket connection management at scale: how to use consistent hashing to
route users to the same chat server, and how to handle server restarts without dropping messages.
Expert Note: Understand Cassandra's data model for messaging: how to design the partition key and
clustering key for efficient message retrieval, and how to handle the 'last message' query efficiently.
Expert Note: Study the CAP theorem implications for messaging: why WhatsApp chose availability over
consistency (messages may be delivered out of order during network partitions), and how sequence
numbers restore order.
Expert Note: Learn about push notification delivery: how APNs and FCM handle offline message delivery,
the difference between high-priority and normal-priority notifications, and how to handle token expiration.
